meta.title: Loan FAQ Dataset (Indian Banking & NBFC)
meta.description: Trilingual loan FAQs for a chatbot/help interface. Use the language block matching the user's input language; match within a block using question text or keywords. Filter by customer_type (All / Salaried / Self-Employed / Professional).
meta.note: General information only, not financial or legal advice.

english.question: What is a CIBIL score?
english.answer: A CIBIL score is a 3-digit number between 300 and 900 that reflects your credit history and repayment behaviour. A higher score means better chances of loan approval. A score of 750 and above is considered very good.

english.question: What is the minimum CIBIL score required for a loan?
english.answer: Most banks prefer a score of 700-750 or above. NBFCs are often more flexible and may lend at 650-700, but usually at a higher interest rate.

english.question: How can I check my CIBIL score for free?
english.answer: You can get one free credit report a year from each bureau (CIBIL, Experian, Equifax, CRIF). Many banks and apps also show your score for free. Checking your own score does not lower it.

english.question: Does checking my score reduce it?
english.answer: No. When you check your own score it is a 'soft enquiry' and has no impact. Your score is only affected when a lender does a 'hard enquiry' on a loan application.

english.question: What should I do if my CIBIL score is low?
english.answer: Pay existing EMIs and credit card bills on time, keep credit utilisation below 30%, don't close old accounts, and avoid applying for many loans at once. Improvement usually takes 3-6 months.

english.question: Can I get a loan with no credit history?
english.answer: Yes, but it can be harder. New applicants can consider a secured loan, a co-applicant/guarantor, or a smaller loan amount. Timely repayment then helps build your credit history.

english.question: What is credit utilisation ratio?
english.answer: It shows how much of your credit card limit you are using. For example, spending 30,000 on a 1 lakh limit is 30% utilisation. Keeping it below 30% is good for your score.

english.question: Does a loan rejection affect my CIBIL score?
english.answer: A rejection itself does not directly lower your score, but each application triggers a 'hard enquiry' that can reduce it slightly. So avoid applying at many places at once.

english.question: What is the difference between settlement and full payment?
english.answer: Full payment means you repaid the entire loan. Settlement means the lender agreed to accept a lower amount - this shows as 'settled' in your report, which is a negative mark. A 'closed/paid' status is always better.

english.question: How do I fix an error in my CIBIL report?
english.answer: You can raise a dispute on the CIBIL website. The bureau usually investigates and corrects it within 30 days. Removing a wrong entry can improve your score.

english.question: Does using a credit card build my score?
english.answer: Yes. Using a credit card responsibly (low utilisation, paying the full bill on time) builds credit history and improves your score. Paying only the minimum due repeatedly is harmful.

english.question: How is credit score judged for self-employed people?
english.answer: For the self-employed, lenders look at both your personal credit score and your business's repayment track record, along with ITRs and bank statements. Consistent income and clean repayment history help a lot.

english.question: Do professionals like doctors and CAs get score-based benefits?
english.answer: Many lenders offer professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, architects) special schemes and better rates, partly because of stable income, but a healthy credit score is still important for the best terms.

english.question: How is loan eligibility decided?
english.answer: Lenders look at your age, income, job or business type, CIBIL score, existing EMIs and repayment capacity. Together these decide your eligibility and the loan amount you can get.

english.question: What minimum salary is needed for a personal loan?
english.answer: In metro cities the minimum is usually around 15,000-25,000 net monthly salary, varying by lender and city. A higher income generally means a higher eligible loan amount.

english.question: What is the age limit for a loan?
english.answer: Most lenders give loans to people aged 21 to 60 (salaried) or up to 65 (self-employed). You must be within the limit until the loan is fully repaid.

english.question: What is FOIR or the EMI-to-income ratio?
english.answer: FOIR is how much of your monthly income goes towards EMIs. Most lenders want total EMIs to stay within 40-50% of your income so that repayment stays safe.

english.question: Can self-employed people get a loan?
english.answer: Yes. For the self-employed, lenders check business proof, ITRs (2-3 years), bank statements and business continuity. Stable income and a good track record make approval easier.

english.question: How many years of business continuity are required?
english.answer: Most lenders want a business vintage of at least 2-3 years with stable or growing income. A longer, consistent track record improves both approval chances and the loan amount.

english.question: Do professionals have separate eligibility criteria?
english.answer: Yes. Qualified professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, architects) often have dedicated schemes where the qualification and practice itself supports eligibility, sometimes with higher amounts and lighter documentation.

english.question: Can a newly qualified doctor or CA get a professional loan?
english.answer: Often yes, but newly qualified professionals may get a smaller amount until they show practice income. Lenders may ask for the degree/registration certificate plus early income proof.

english.question: Will I get a loan right after changing jobs?
english.answer: Most lenders want a minimum of 6 months to 1 year in your current job and 1-2 years of total experience. Approval right after joining a brand-new job can be difficult.

english.question: Can people paid in cash get a loan?
english.answer: It is difficult because there is no income proof. If salary or income is credited to a bank account it becomes proof. NBFCs sometimes consider alternative proof.

english.question: Can a pensioner get a loan?
english.answer: Yes, many banks offer special loans to pensioners if the pension is credited regularly to a bank account. Age limits and amount restrictions may apply.

english.question: How can I improve my eligibility?
english.answer: Add a co-applicant, close small existing loans, keep strong income proof, and improve your CIBIL score. This improves both the loan amount and the approval odds.

english.question: Can I take more than one loan at the same time?
english.answer: Technically yes, if your income and FOIR allow it. But more EMIs increase repayment pressure and can make future loans harder to get.

english.question: Can a homemaker or student get a loan?
english.answer: Without income a direct loan is difficult. Options include a co-applicant/guarantor or a secured loan (against FD or gold). Students have a separate education loan category.

english.question: What documents are needed for a personal loan?
english.answer: Usually: PAN card, Aadhaar (ID/address proof), latest salary slips (3 months), bank statements (3-6 months) and a photo. Self-employed also need ITRs and business proof.

english.question: Is a PAN card mandatory for a loan?
english.answer: Yes, a PAN card is required for almost every loan because it links to your income and credit history. Without it the application is hard to process.

english.question: What can I use as address proof?
english.answer: Aadhaar, passport, voter ID, driving licence, or a recent utility bill (electricity/gas/water) work as address proof. The name and address should be current.

english.question: What counts as income proof for salaried people?
english.answer: For salaried applicants, salary slips (usually 3 months) and bank statements showing salary credits are the main income proof. Form 16 or an employment letter may also be asked for.

english.question: What counts as income proof for self-employed people?
english.answer: For the self-employed, ITRs (2-3 years), profit & loss statements, GST returns and business bank statements act as income proof. These show the stability of your business income.

english.question: What extra documents do professionals need?
english.answer: Professionals usually need their qualification/degree certificate and practice registration (e.g. medical council, ICAI, bar council) in addition to ITRs and bank statements, as this supports a professional-category loan.

english.question: How many months of bank statements are needed?
english.answer: Usually the latest 3 to 6 months of bank statements are needed, showing salary or business income credits. This verifies income stability.

english.question: Can I submit documents online?
english.answer: Yes, most lenders now allow digital uploads. Aadhaar-based e-KYC makes the process faster, and sometimes paperless approval is possible.

english.question: What can the self-employed do without ITRs?
english.answer: Approval is hard without ITRs. You can provide GST returns, bank statements, or business proof, but filing ITRs is strongly advised as it also helps future loans.

english.question: Are the guarantor's documents also needed?
english.answer: If the loan has a guarantor/co-applicant, their ID, address and income proof are also required, since they are equally responsible for repayment.

english.question: What is the interest rate on a personal loan?
english.answer: Usually 10% to 24% per annum, depending on your CIBIL score, income and lender. NBFC rates can be slightly higher than bank rates.

english.question: What is the difference between fixed and floating interest rates?
english.answer: A fixed rate stays the same for the whole tenure, so the EMI is predictable. A floating rate changes with the market - it can go down or up. Personal loans are usually fixed.

english.question: How can I get a lower interest rate?
english.answer: A good CIBIL score (750+), stable income, an existing relationship with the bank, and comparing multiple offers to negotiate all help you get a lower rate.

english.question: What is the difference between a flat rate and a reducing rate?
english.answer: A flat rate is calculated on the full principal (more expensive). A reducing rate applies only to the outstanding balance (cheaper). A reducing-balance loan is always better.

english.question: What is APR?
english.answer: APR (Annual Percentage Rate) combines the interest rate plus processing fees and other charges into the true yearly cost. Comparing APR is the best way to compare loans.

english.question: Can the interest rate be negotiated?
english.answer: Yes. If your score is good, your salary account is with the same bank, or another lender has a better offer, you can request a rate reduction.

english.question: Do professionals get lower interest rates?
english.answer: Often yes. Many lenders offer doctors, CAs and similar professionals preferential rates because of perceived income stability, but the final rate still depends on your profile and score.

english.question: Is the rate lower on a pre-approved loan?
english.answer: Often yes, because the bank already has your history. Even so, always compare the final rate and charges before accepting.

english.question: What is the difference between secured and unsecured loans?
english.answer: A secured loan requires collateral (house, vehicle, FD) and has a lower rate. An unsecured loan (like a personal loan) is given without collateral and has a higher rate.

english.question: What can a personal loan be used for?
english.answer: A personal loan is multi-purpose - wedding, medical emergency, travel, home renovation, education or any need. The lender usually does not ask for usage details.

english.question: What is the difference between a home loan and a personal loan?
english.answer: A home loan is for property, with a long tenure (20-30 years), lower rate, and the property as collateral. A personal loan is for any purpose, with a short tenure, higher rate, and no collateral.

english.question: What is a top-up loan?
english.answer: If you have an existing loan (like a home loan) with a good repayment record, the lender can give an extra amount as a 'top-up', usually at a lower rate.

english.question: How does a gold loan work?
english.answer: You pledge your gold and get a loan based on its value (usually up to 75%). The rate is low and approval is fast. You get the gold back when the loan is repaid.

english.question: What is an overdraft loan?
english.answer: In an overdraft you get a limit and withdraw only what you need. Interest applies only on the used amount and only for the days used. It is useful for business cash flow.

english.question: How do I get a business loan?
english.answer: For a business loan, lenders check business vintage, turnover, ITR/GST returns and bank statements. It can be used for working capital, expansion or equipment.

english.question: What is a working capital loan?
english.answer: A working capital loan funds day-to-day business needs like inventory, salaries and short-term expenses. It is usually short-term and may be given as a cash credit or overdraft facility.

english.question: What is a loan against property (LAP)?
english.answer: You pledge your property to get a large loan that can be used for any purpose. The rate is lower than a personal loan because the property acts as collateral.

english.question: What is required for an education loan?
english.answer: Admission proof, course/fee details, a co-applicant (usually a parent) and collateral (for large amounts). Many education loans have a moratorium - no EMI until studies finish.

english.question: What is a consumer durable loan?
english.answer: It is given for products like phones, TVs and fridges, often on EMI (sometimes 'no-cost EMI'). The process is fast and approval is available right at the point of sale.

english.question: What is an instant or payday loan?
english.answer: It is a small, short-term loan disbursed very fast, but the interest and charges are quite high. Use it only for a genuine emergency and only from an RBI-registered lender.

english.question: What is a professional loan for doctors and CAs?
english.answer: It is a tailored loan for qualified professionals to set up or expand their clinic, office or practice. It often needs lighter documentation and may offer higher amounts based on the qualification.

english.question: What is a vehicle or car loan?
english.answer: A vehicle loan funds the purchase of a car or two-wheeler, with the vehicle itself as collateral. Tenure is usually 1-7 years and rates are lower than a personal loan.

english.question: What is an EMI?
english.answer: An EMI (Equated Monthly Installment) is the fixed amount you pay every month to repay a loan. It includes both principal and interest.

english.question: How is an EMI calculated?
english.answer: An EMI depends on the loan amount, interest rate and tenure. A bigger amount or higher rate means a bigger EMI. A longer tenure means a smaller EMI but more total interest. An online EMI calculator works it out easily.

english.question: What happens if I miss an EMI?
english.answer: A late fee/penalty is charged, your CIBIL score drops, and repeated misses can lead to default. If you face difficulty, talk to your lender in advance.

english.question: Can the EMI date be changed?
english.answer: Many lenders let you change the EMI date so it matches your salary date. You need to request this from the lender.

english.question: What charge applies if an EMI bounces?
english.answer: A bounce/dishonour charge (usually 300-750 per bounce) plus a late payment penalty applies. Repeated bounces also negatively affect your CIBIL score.

english.question: Should I choose a longer or shorter tenure?
english.answer: A shorter tenure means a bigger EMI but less total interest. A longer tenure means a smaller EMI but more total interest. Balance it against your monthly capacity.

english.question: Is no-cost EMI really free?
english.answer: In a 'no-cost EMI' the interest is often adjusted into the product price or discount. Technically, under RBI norms, interest is never truly free - always check the total cost.

english.question: What do I pay besides the EMI?
english.answer: Processing fee, GST, and sometimes insurance or stamp duty. The EMI is only loan repayment - treat these one-time charges separately.

english.question: What is pre-EMI?
english.answer: In some loans (like for an under-construction property) you pay only the interest before the full EMI begins - this is called pre-EMI. The principal starts later.

english.question: Can the self-employed get flexible or step-up EMIs?
english.answer: Some lenders offer flexible or step-up EMI plans where EMIs start lower and rise later, which suits businesses with seasonal or growing income. Availability depends on the lender.

english.question: What is a processing fee?
english.answer: It is a one-time charge the lender takes to process the application, usually 0.5% to 3% of the loan amount plus GST. It can sometimes be negotiated or waived.

english.question: What is a prepayment or foreclosure charge?
english.answer: If you repay the loan before the term ends, some lenders charge a fee (usually 2-5% of the outstanding). On floating-rate loans this charge is often not allowed under RBI rules.

english.question: What is part-payment?
english.answer: You can pay an extra amount beyond the EMI to reduce the loan principal, which lowers future interest and tenure. Some lenders set limits or charges on this.

english.question: How do I avoid hidden charges?
english.answer: Read the loan agreement and sanction letter carefully, ask for the full Schedule of Charges, and ask for the APR. Get everything in writing.

english.question: Where does GST apply on a loan?
english.answer: GST applies not on the loan interest but on the fees/charges (like processing fee, bounce charge), usually at 18%.

english.question: Is there a charge for cancelling a loan?
english.answer: If you cancel after disbursal, some lenders may keep the processing fee and charge interest for the days used. Cancelling within the cooling-off period is cheaper.

english.question: Does stamp duty apply to a loan?
english.answer: Some loans (especially secured loans) may carry a state-dependent stamp duty. It is usually a small one-time charge.

english.question: Is loan insurance mandatory?
english.answer: In most cases it is optional, though the lender may suggest it. It covers the borrower in case of death/disability. You can decide whether to take it - don't treat it as mandatory.

english.question: How do I apply for a loan?
english.answer: You can apply online via the lender's website/app, or offline at a branch. Fill the form, upload documents, and after verification you get approval.

english.question: How long does loan approval take?
english.answer: Personal/pre-approved loans take a few hours to 2-3 days. Secured loans (home/property) can take 1-3 weeks due to verification.

english.question: How is the loan disbursed?
english.answer: After approval and agreement, the amount is transferred straight to your bank account. In some cases (like a home loan) it goes to the seller/builder.

english.question: Is applying for a loan online safe?
english.answer: Yes, if you use the official website/app. Never share OTP or details on an unknown link, random SMS or call. Only borrow from an RBI-registered lender.

english.question: What is a sanction letter?
english.answer: It is an official document stating the loan amount, interest rate, tenure, EMI and charges. Read it carefully before signing.

english.question: How do I track my loan application?
english.answer: The lender gives you an application/reference number. Use it to check status via the website, app or customer care.

english.question: What are the benefits of adding a co-applicant?
english.answer: A co-applicant increases combined income, improving the loan amount and approval odds. Both are responsible for repayment.

english.question: What should I do if my loan is rejected?
english.answer: Ask the reason (low score, high FOIR, or documents). Fix it, wait a few months, then reapply. Avoid applying at many places at once.

english.question: What is a loan's cooling-off period?
english.answer: It is a short window (usually a few days) in which you can cancel the loan with little or no penalty, paying only interest for the days used. RBI has made this mandatory for digital loans.

english.question: Why do lenders verify employment?
english.answer: Lenders verify employment to confirm income stability and job continuity, which affects your repayment capacity. They may call HR or check salary slips and bank credits.

english.question: What are the benefits of repaying a loan early?
english.answer: Early repayment reduces the total interest and makes you debt-free sooner. Just compare any foreclosure charge against the interest you would save.

english.question: What should I collect after a loan is closed?
english.answer: Always collect a No Objection Certificate (NOC) or closure letter, and ensure the loan shows as 'closed' in your CIBIL report. For secured loans, collect the collateral/papers back.

english.question: What is a loan balance transfer?
english.answer: You can shift your loan to another lender at a lower rate. This can reduce the EMI or total interest, but always count the transfer/processing charges.

english.question: What is auto-debit (NACH)?
english.answer: With NACH your EMI is automatically deducted from your bank account each month, reducing the risk of missing it. Keep enough balance so it doesn't bounce.

english.question: Can I pay manually instead of auto-debit?
english.answer: Yes, you can usually pay via net banking, UPI or at a branch. But auto-debit is safer so you don't miss the due date.

english.question: What is loan restructuring or a moratorium?
english.answer: In financial difficulty the lender may let you lower the EMI, extend the tenure, or pause EMIs for a while (a moratorium). You must request this from the lender - it is not automatic.

english.question: What happens if I default on a loan?
english.answer: Continued non-payment leads to penalties, a damaged credit score, recovery follow-ups, and for secured loans the lender may take the collateral. Always contact the lender early to find a solution.

english.question: What is the difference between an NBFC and a bank?
english.answer: Banks are RBI-licensed and can accept deposits. NBFCs are also RBI-registered but don't offer savings-account-type facilities. NBFCs are often faster and more flexible, but rates can be slightly higher.

english.question: Is it safe to take a loan from an NBFC?
english.answer: Yes, if the NBFC is RBI-registered. Before applying, check its registration on the RBI website and look at reviews. Stay away from unregistered lenders.

english.question: Does an NBFC give a loan faster?
english.answer: Usually yes. NBFCs have a digital and flexible process, so approval/disbursal is fast - especially for small-ticket loans.

english.question: Do NBFCs lend at lower CIBIL scores?
english.answer: Some NBFCs are more flexible than banks and may lend at lower scores, but at a higher interest rate. Read the terms carefully.

english.question: Are a loan app and an NBFC the same thing?
english.answer: Not necessarily. Many loan apps are partners of an NBFC. Always check that an RBI-registered lender is behind the app, otherwise there is a fraud risk.

english.question: How do I avoid loan fraud?
english.answer: Never pay an advance fee for a loan, never share OTP/CVV/PIN, and borrow only from an RBI-registered lender. Be wary of 'guaranteed approval' or 'no documents' offers.

english.question: How do I spot a fake loan app?
english.answer: Instant loans without KYC, excessive permissions (contacts/photos), hidden charges, and threatening recovery are all red flags. Use only trusted, RBI-linked apps.

english.question: Is it right to pay an advance fee for a loan?
english.answer: No. A genuine lender takes the processing fee from the loan amount or transparently, not as advance cash before approval. Demanding an advance fee is often a scam.

english.question: What if a recovery agent misbehaves?
english.answer: Under RBI rules, recovery agents must contact you respectfully and only within set hours (8 am to 7 pm). If harassed, complain to the lender's grievance cell or the RBI Ombudsman.

english.question: Is my data safe with a loan app?
english.answer: RBI-registered lenders must follow data privacy rules. Read the privacy policy before applying and don't give the app more permissions than necessary.

english.question: What is the RBI Ombudsman?
english.answer: It is a free complaint system where you can complain against a bank/NBFC if they don't resolve your problem. You must first complain to the lender's grievance cell.

english.question: What if someone asks for an OTP for a loan?
english.answer: Never tell anyone your OTP, PIN, CVV or password - banks/NBFCs never ask for these on the phone. Treat any call/SMS asking for an OTP as a scam and disconnect immediately.

english.question: What should I think about before taking a loan?
english.answer: Check your real need and repayment capacity, understand the total cost (interest + charges), and borrow only what you can comfortably repay. Don't take more than you need.

english.question: How do I find the best loan deal?
english.answer: Compare offers from multiple lenders - interest rate (reducing), processing fee, foreclosure charge and APR. Look at the total cost, not just the EMI.

english.question: How much loan should I take?
english.answer: Only as much as you actually need, keeping EMIs below 40-50% of your income. More loan means more interest and financial stress.

english.question: What must I read in a loan agreement?
english.answer: Interest rate and type, total charges, EMI and tenure, prepayment/foreclosure rules, and late payment penalties. If anything is unclear, ask before signing.

english.question: Should I take a loan even if I have an emergency fund?
english.answer: For small needs, using the emergency fund is better as it saves interest. For large or planned expenses, a loan is fine. Keep a balance between the two.

english.question: Is it wise to take a loan on a festival or instant offer?
english.answer: The offer may be good, but don't decide in a hurry. Check the charges and total cost the same way as a normal loan. A tempting offer can hide costs.

english.question: Credit card loan vs personal loan - which is better?
english.answer: Cash withdrawal/EMI on a credit card is expensive. For a large amount or longer need, a personal loan is often cheaper. Compare the rate and charges of both.

english.question: Where can I ask loan-related questions?
english.answer: From your lender's customer care, branch, or official website. Always take information from a verified source and never share personal/bank details with an unknown caller.

english.question: Should I prepay the loan or invest the money?
english.answer: If your loan interest is higher than what your investment would earn, prepaying usually wins. If investments earn more and your loan rate is low, investing may be better. Consider taxes and your comfort with debt. This is general information, not financial advice.

english.question: What loan tips help self-employed borrowers?
english.answer: File ITRs regularly, keep business and personal accounts separate, maintain clean bank statements, and time your loan when income is stable. This strengthens your profile and gets better terms.

english.question: What loan tips help professionals?
english.answer: Use professional-category schemes, keep your practice registration and ITRs ready, and compare lenders who specialise in professional loans. Your qualification can fetch higher amounts and lower rates.

english.question: What loan tips help salaried borrowers?
english.answer: Keep salary credited to one bank account, maintain a stable job record, take a loan from your salary-account bank for better rates, and keep total EMIs within a comfortable share of your salary.

english.question: Are there tax benefits on loans?
english.answer: Some loans give tax benefits - for example, home loan interest and principal qualify under certain sections, and education loan interest is deductible. A personal loan generally has no tax benefit unless used for specific purposes. Consult a tax advisor for your case.

english.question: Why are ITRs so important for self-employed loans?
english.answer: ITRs are the main proof of income for the self-employed. Lenders usually need 2-3 years of ITRs to assess income stability and decide the loan amount. Regular filing greatly improves loan access.

english.question: Does GST registration help in getting a business loan?
english.answer: Yes. GST returns provide verifiable turnover proof, which many lenders use to assess business loans. Consistent GST filings strengthen your application.

english.question: Do professionals get tax benefits on a professional loan?
english.answer: If a professional loan is used for business/practice purposes, the interest may be claimed as a business expense. The exact treatment depends on usage and tax rules - check with a tax advisor.

english.question: Can I show a loan as income in my ITR?
english.answer: No, a loan is not income - it is borrowed money you have to repay, so it is not taxable as income. However, lenders look at your ITR to verify the income you earn separately.

english.question: I am on probation - can I get a personal loan?
english.answer: It can be difficult during probation because lenders prefer a confirmed, stable job. Some lenders may still consider it with a strong score and salary. Waiting until confirmation usually improves your chances.

english.question: My salary is partly in cash - how does that affect my loan?
english.answer: Lenders count only the income credited to your bank account as verifiable. Cash salary that doesn't reflect in statements usually can't be counted, which may lower your eligible amount.

english.question: Can I add my spouse's salary to increase the loan amount?
english.answer: Yes, adding your spouse as a co-applicant combines both incomes, which can increase the eligible loan amount. Both then share repayment responsibility.

english.question: Does a high existing EMI reduce my new loan eligibility?
english.answer: Yes. Existing EMIs raise your FOIR, leaving less room for a new EMI. Closing small loans before applying can improve your eligibility for a new loan.

english.question: My business income fluctuates - how do lenders assess it?
english.answer: Lenders look at average income over 2-3 years of ITRs and bank statements rather than a single month. Stable averages and healthy bank balances reassure them despite month-to-month swings.

english.question: Can I get a loan against my business turnover?
english.answer: Yes, several lenders offer loans based on turnover (often verified via GST returns and bank statements). Higher, consistent turnover usually means a higher eligible amount.

english.question: What is a loan for sole proprietors vs partnership firms?
english.answer: For a sole proprietor, the owner's and business's finances are assessed together. For a partnership/company, the firm's financials and sometimes partners' guarantees are considered. Documentation differs accordingly.

english.question: My business is less than 2 years old - can I still get a loan?
english.answer: It is harder, as most lenders want 2-3 years of vintage. Options include a smaller amount, a secured loan, a co-applicant with stable income, or some NBFCs that consider newer businesses.

english.question: Should I take a personal loan or a business loan?
english.answer: If the money is for the business (stock, equipment, expansion), a business loan often suits better and may have tax benefits on interest. For personal needs, a personal loan is appropriate. Match the loan type to the actual use.

english.question: I am a doctor setting up a clinic - what loan suits me?
english.answer: A professional loan or equipment/business loan suits clinic set-up, covering interiors, medical equipment and working capital. Many lenders have doctor-specific schemes with higher limits and lighter documentation.

english.question: I am a CA/lawyer - can I get a loan based on my practice?
english.answer: Yes. Lenders offer professional loans based on your qualification, practice registration and ITRs. A well-established practice with steady income usually gets higher amounts and better rates.

english.question: Can I get a loan to buy equipment for my practice?
english.answer: Yes, an equipment or professional loan can fund machines, instruments or office set-up. Some lenders fund a high percentage of the equipment cost and align EMIs with your practice income.

english.question: Do I need collateral for a professional loan?
english.answer: Many professional loans are unsecured (no collateral) up to a certain amount, relying on your qualification and income. Larger amounts may need collateral. Terms vary by lender.

english.question: What is the difference between a co-applicant and a guarantor?
english.answer: A co-applicant shares the loan and repayment from the start and the loan reflects on their credit too. A guarantor steps in only if the borrower fails to pay, but is still legally responsible.

english.question: Who can be a co-applicant?
english.answer: Usually a close family member - spouse, parent, child or sibling. The co-applicant should ideally have a stable income and a good credit score to strengthen the application.

english.question: Does being a guarantor affect my own credit score?
english.answer: Yes, it can. If the borrower defaults, the guarantor's credit score and liability are affected. Only become a guarantor for someone you trust and whose repayment ability you are confident about.

english.question: Can I remove a co-applicant later?
english.answer: Removing a co-applicant mid-loan is difficult and needs the lender's approval, usually only if the remaining applicant alone qualifies for the loan. The lender re-evaluates eligibility.

english.question: Personal loan vs gold loan - which should I choose?
english.answer: A gold loan usually has a lower rate and faster approval but needs you to pledge gold. A personal loan needs no collateral but has a higher rate. Choose based on whether you can pledge gold and how fast you need funds.

english.question: Personal loan vs loan against property - which is better for a large amount?
english.answer: For a large amount, a loan against property usually offers a lower rate and longer tenure but needs property as collateral and takes longer. A personal loan is faster and collateral-free but costlier for large sums.

english.question: Should I take one big loan or several small ones?
english.answer: One well-managed loan is usually simpler and cheaper to track than several small ones, which can have multiple fees and EMIs. Consolidating needs into one loan often reduces total cost and confusion.

english.question: Fixed vs floating rate - which should I pick?
english.answer: Pick fixed if you want predictable EMIs and expect rates to rise. Pick floating if you expect rates to fall and can handle some variation. For short-tenure personal loans the choice matters less; for long home loans it matters more.

english.question: Bank vs NBFC - which should I choose for a loan?
english.answer: Choose a bank for typically lower rates and a long-term relationship, especially with a good score. Choose an NBFC for faster processing, flexibility, or if a bank declines you - accepting a possibly higher rate. Always verify RBI registration.

english.question: Can the loan be disbursed to a different account?
english.answer: Usually the loan is disbursed to your own verified bank account. Some lenders allow a chosen account, but for security the account is typically in the borrower's name.

english.question: What is partial disbursal?
english.answer: Partial disbursal means the lender releases the loan in stages rather than all at once - common in home loans for under-construction property, where money is released as construction progresses.

english.question: When does my EMI start after disbursal?
english.answer: Usually the first EMI starts the month after disbursal, on your chosen EMI date. The exact start is mentioned in your sanction letter and repayment schedule.

english.question: How many loans is too many?
english.answer: There is no fixed number, but if your total EMIs cross 40-50% of income, or you struggle to track payments, that is too many. Lenders also see many active loans as higher risk.

english.question: Does closing a credit card hurt my score?
english.answer: It can, because closing a card lowers your total available credit and can raise your utilisation ratio, and it shortens your credit history. Often it is better to keep an old card active with low usage.

english.question: How long does negative information stay on my credit report?
english.answer: Negative entries like defaults or settlements typically stay on your report for up to 7 years. Consistent good repayment afterwards gradually reduces their impact on your score.

english.question: Does paying only the minimum due on a credit card affect my loan chances?
english.answer: Yes. Paying only the minimum keeps high outstanding balances and interest, raises your utilisation, and signals repayment stress - which can lower your score and hurt loan approval.

english.question: What is the principal of a loan?
english.answer: The principal is the original amount you borrow, before interest. Your EMI repays both this principal and the interest charged on it over the tenure.

english.question: What is loan tenure?
english.answer: Tenure is the time period over which you repay the loan, expressed in months or years. A longer tenure lowers the EMI but increases total interest paid.

english.question: What is an amortisation schedule?
english.answer: An amortisation schedule is a table showing each EMI split into principal and interest over the tenure. Early EMIs are mostly interest; later EMIs are mostly principal.

english.question: What is collateral?
english.answer: Collateral is an asset (like property, gold or an FD) you pledge to secure a loan. If you can't repay, the lender can use the collateral to recover the dues. Secured loans have collateral; unsecured loans don't.

english.question: What does loan disbursal mean?
english.answer: Disbursal is when the approved loan amount is actually released and credited to your (or the seller's) account. The loan officially becomes active and the repayment schedule begins.

english.question: How much home loan can I get on my income?
english.answer: Lenders usually fund up to about 75-90% of the property value, with the loan amount also limited by your income so that EMIs stay within 40-50% of it. A higher income and longer tenure increase the amount.

english.question: What is the down payment for a home loan?
english.answer: The down payment is the part of the property cost you pay yourself, usually around 10-25%. The lender finances the rest. A larger down payment means a smaller loan and lower interest.

english.question: What is a home loan balance transfer and when is it worth it?
english.answer: It means moving your home loan to another lender at a lower rate. It is worth it when the rate saving is large and enough tenure remains, after counting transfer and processing charges.

english.question: Can I get a joint home loan with my spouse?
english.answer: Yes. A joint home loan combines both incomes for a higher eligible amount, and co-owners may each claim applicable tax benefits. Both are responsible for repayment.

english.question: What property documents are checked for a home loan?
english.answer: Lenders verify the title deed, sale agreement, approved building plan, and that the property is free of legal disputes. A clear title is essential for approval.

english.question: How do self-employed people qualify for a home loan?
english.answer: Through ITRs (2-3 years), business proof, bank statements and computation of income. A stable business and good repayment history help get a higher amount and better rate.

english.question: How much of a car's price can I get as a loan?
english.answer: Lenders usually fund about 80-90% of the on-road price; you pay the rest as down payment. A higher down payment lowers the EMI and total interest.

english.question: Is the interest rate different for a used car loan?
english.answer: Yes. Used-car loans usually have a higher interest rate and shorter tenure than new-car loans, because used vehicles carry more risk and depreciate faster.

english.question: What happens to the vehicle if I don't repay the loan?
english.answer: In a vehicle loan the car is hypothecated to the lender. If you default, the lender can repossess the vehicle to recover the dues. The hypothecation is removed once the loan is fully repaid.

english.question: Can I get a two-wheeler loan with a low income?
english.answer: Often yes - two-wheeler loans have smaller amounts and easier eligibility. A reasonable income, basic documents and a fair credit score are usually enough.

english.question: What is a moratorium period in an education loan?
english.answer: It is a period during your course (plus usually 6-12 months after) when you don't have to pay full EMIs. Interest may still accrue, so paying simple interest during this time reduces the later burden.

english.question: Do I need collateral for an education loan?
english.answer: Small education loans are often collateral-free; larger amounts (especially for studying abroad) usually need collateral plus a co-applicant. Exact limits vary by lender and scheme.

english.question: Are there tax benefits on an education loan?
english.answer: Interest paid on an education loan is deductible under a specific section for a number of years from when repayment starts. The principal is not deductible. Check current rules with a tax advisor.

english.question: How is the gold loan amount decided?
english.answer: It is based on the weight and purity of your gold and the RBI-allowed loan-to-value ratio (up to about 75%). Higher purity and weight give a higher loan amount.

english.question: Is my gold safe with the lender?
english.answer: Reputable, RBI-registered lenders store pledged gold in secure, insured vaults and return it on full repayment. Always use a trusted lender and keep the pledge receipt safe.

english.question: What happens to my gold if I can't repay?
english.answer: If you default, after notices the lender can auction the pledged gold to recover dues. Any surplus after recovery is returned to you. Repaying or renewing on time avoids this.

english.question: What is KYC for a loan?
english.answer: KYC (Know Your Customer) is identity and address verification using documents like Aadhaar and PAN. It is mandatory for every loan to prevent fraud and comply with RBI rules.

english.question: What is e-KYC and is it faster?
english.answer: e-KYC verifies your identity digitally (often via Aadhaar OTP), making loan approval faster and paperless. It is convenient but should only be done on the lender's official platform.

english.question: Do I need to do KYC again for a new loan with the same bank?
english.answer: If your KYC is already updated and valid, you may not need to repeat the full process. But the lender may ask you to re-verify if details have changed or KYC is due for periodic update.

english.question: What is loan protection insurance?
english.answer: It is a cover that repays the outstanding loan if the borrower dies or becomes disabled, protecting the family from the debt burden. It is usually optional - you can choose whether to buy it.

english.question: Is it mandatory to buy insurance offered with a loan?
english.answer: No, bundled insurance is generally optional and you should not be forced to buy it to get the loan. Decide based on your need, and ask for the cost separately from the loan.

english.question: How do I file a complaint against a lender?
english.answer: First complain to the lender's customer care/grievance cell and keep the complaint reference. If unresolved within the set time, you can escalate to the RBI Ombudsman through the official RBI portal.

english.question: What can I complain about to the RBI Ombudsman?
english.answer: You can complain about issues like wrongful charges, non-closure of a loan, harassment by recovery agents, or non-correction of credit report errors, after the lender fails to resolve them. The service is free.

english.question: What are my rights as a borrower?
english.answer: You have the right to clear information on rates and charges, fair treatment, privacy of your data, respectful recovery practices, and a grievance redressal process. Lenders must follow RBI's fair practices code.

english.question: How often is my CIBIL score updated?
english.answer: Lenders report to the bureaus monthly, so your score typically updates every 30-45 days. Changes from a payment or new loan may take a cycle or two to reflect.

english.question: What is a good vs excellent CIBIL score range?
english.answer: Roughly: below 650 is weak, 650-749 is fair, 750-799 is good, and 800+ is excellent. Higher ranges usually mean easier approval and better interest rates.

english.question: Do multiple loan enquiries in a short time hurt my score?
english.answer: Yes. Several hard enquiries in a short span signal credit hunger and can lower your score. Space out applications and compare offers without formally applying everywhere.

english.question: Can I improve my score quickly before applying for a loan?
english.answer: There is no instant fix, but you can pay down credit card balances to lower utilisation, clear overdue amounts, and avoid new enquiries. Visible improvement usually takes a few months.

english.question: Does my income affect my CIBIL score?
english.answer: No, income is not part of the score itself - the score is based on credit behaviour. But lenders look at income separately, alongside the score, to judge repayment capacity.

english.question: Does my city or location affect loan eligibility?
english.answer: It can. Income thresholds and eligible amounts may differ between metro and non-metro areas because of cost of living and risk assessment. Some lenders also lend only in serviceable locations.

english.question: Can I get a loan if I already have a home loan running?
english.answer: Yes, if your income supports both EMIs within the FOIR limit. A well-paid existing home loan can even reflect positively, but the new EMI must fit your repayment capacity.

english.question: Does my professional qualification increase my loan eligibility?
english.answer: Often yes. A recognised qualification (medical, CA, law, engineering) can raise eligibility under professional schemes, as lenders view such professions as stable and creditworthy.

english.question: What is the maximum loan amount I can get?
english.answer: It depends on your income, repayment capacity (FOIR), credit score, loan type and any collateral. For unsecured loans it is usually a multiple of monthly income; for secured loans it depends on the asset value.

english.question: What is the difference between part-prepayment and foreclosure?
english.answer: Part-prepayment is paying an extra lump sum while the loan continues, reducing principal and future interest. Foreclosure is paying off the entire outstanding to close the loan early.

english.question: Is it better to reduce the EMI or the tenure when I part-prepay?
english.answer: Reducing the tenure (keeping the EMI same) usually saves more total interest. Reducing the EMI eases monthly cash flow. Choose based on whether you want lower cost or lower monthly outgo.

english.question: When are foreclosure charges not allowed?
english.answer: Under RBI rules, foreclosure/prepayment charges are generally not allowed on floating-rate loans taken by individuals. Fixed-rate loans may still carry such charges, so check your agreement.

english.question: What is digital lending?
english.answer: Digital lending is borrowing through an app or website where application, KYC, approval and disbursal happen online, often within minutes. Always use an RBI-regulated platform.

english.question: How do I know a lending app is RBI-regulated?
english.answer: Check that the app names its RBI-registered lender/NBFC partner, has a clear privacy policy and grievance contact, and appears on the lender's official channels. Avoid apps that hide the lender's identity.

english.question: What is a Key Fact Statement (KFS)?
english.answer: A KFS is a standard document that clearly lists the loan's interest rate, all charges, APR and repayment terms in one place. RBI requires lenders to give it so you can compare loans transparently.

english.question: Why is my interest rate higher than the advertised rate?
english.answer: Advertised rates are usually the lowest, offered to top-profile borrowers. Your actual rate depends on your credit score, income, loan amount and tenure, so it can be higher than the headline figure.

english.question: What is a repo-linked interest rate?
english.answer: A repo-linked rate moves with the RBI's repo rate, so when the RBI changes the repo rate, your floating loan rate adjusts accordingly. It makes rate changes more transparent for borrowers.

english.question: Does a longer tenure increase the interest rate?
english.answer: The rate itself may not change much, but a longer tenure means you pay interest for more months, so the total interest paid is higher even if the EMI is smaller.

english.question: Can I pay my EMI through UPI?
english.answer: Many lenders now accept EMI payments via UPI, net banking or their app, besides auto-debit. Check your lender's accepted modes and always keep proof of payment.

english.question: What is an EMI bounce and how do I avoid it?
english.answer: An EMI bounce happens when the auto-debit fails due to insufficient balance. Avoid it by keeping enough balance before the EMI date and setting a reminder a couple of days ahead.

english.question: Can I increase my EMI to close the loan faster?
english.answer: Many lenders allow increasing the EMI (or making part-prepayments), which reduces tenure and total interest. Confirm the option and any conditions with your lender.

english.question: Myth: Checking my own credit score lowers it. True or false?
english.answer: False. Checking your own score is a soft enquiry and never lowers it. Only a lender's hard enquiry on an application can have a small effect.

english.question: Myth: A high income guarantees loan approval. True or false?
english.answer: False. Income matters, but approval also depends on your credit score, existing EMIs (FOIR), documents and stability. A high earner with a poor score can still be declined.

english.question: Myth: Closing a loan early always saves money. True or false?
english.answer: Mostly true, but check for foreclosure charges. If the charge is small compared to the interest saved, early closure helps. On floating-rate individual loans such charges are often not allowed.

english.question: Myth: NBFCs are not safe compared to banks. True or false?
english.answer: False if the NBFC is RBI-registered. Registered NBFCs are regulated and can be safe; the key is to verify registration and read the terms, rather than judging by the NBFC label alone.

english.question: What if my name is spelled differently across documents?
english.answer: Name mismatches across PAN, Aadhaar and bank records can delay approval. Get the spellings corrected to match, or provide a supporting affidavit/proof so the lender can reconcile them.

english.question: Is Form 16 necessary for a personal loan?
english.answer: Not always, but Form 16 is useful proof of salary and tax deducted for salaried applicants. If you don't have it, salary slips and bank statements can often serve as income proof.

english.question: What is a cancelled cheque used for in a loan?
english.answer: A cancelled cheque is used to verify your bank account details for disbursal and to set up auto-debit (NACH) for EMIs. It confirms the account number and IFSC.

english.question: What is a debt consolidation loan?
english.answer: It combines multiple existing debts into one loan with a single EMI, ideally at a lower rate. It simplifies repayment and can reduce total interest, if managed well.

english.question: What is a loan against fixed deposit?
english.answer: You can borrow against your FD without breaking it, usually up to 90-95% of its value, at a rate slightly above the FD rate. The FD keeps earning interest while it serves as security.

english.question: What is a pre-approved loan and why do I get such offers?
english.answer: A pre-approved loan is an offer the lender extends based on your existing relationship, salary credits and credit history. It usually means faster processing, but you should still compare the final rate and charges.

english.question: What is a microfinance loan?
english.answer: A microfinance loan is a small loan, often given to low-income individuals or self-help groups for livelihood or small business, sometimes without traditional collateral. It is regulated to keep terms fair.

english.question: What is loan underwriting?
english.answer: Underwriting is the lender's process of assessing your risk - checking income, score, documents and capacity - to decide whether to approve the loan and on what terms.

english.question: What is a field verification visit?
english.answer: Some lenders send an agent to verify your home or office address and details in person. Keep documents ready and be available, as failed verification can delay or stop approval.

english.question: Can I withdraw my loan application after applying?
english.answer: Yes, you can usually withdraw before disbursal. After disbursal you would need to cancel/foreclose, which may involve charges or interest for the used period. Inform the lender promptly.

english.question: How long does it take to get my CIBIL updated after closing a loan?
english.answer: After closure, the lender reports to the bureaus in the next monthly cycle, so it can take up to 30-45 days for the 'closed' status to reflect. Keep the NOC until it updates.

english.question: What if the lender does not give me a NOC after closure?
english.answer: First request it in writing from customer care. If still not provided, escalate to the grievance cell and then, if needed, the RBI Ombudsman. A NOC is your right after full repayment.

english.question: Can I reduce my EMI burden without closing the loan?
english.answer: Yes - options include extending the tenure, a balance transfer to a lower rate, or part-prepayment to lower the EMI. Discuss with your lender, and weigh any charges against the savings.

english.question: Someone is offering a loan with 'guaranteed approval' - is it genuine?
english.answer: Be very cautious. No genuine lender guarantees approval without checking your profile. 'Guaranteed approval', 'no documents' and upfront-fee demands are classic scam signs. Verify the lender first.

english.question: I think I was a victim of loan fraud - what should I do?
english.answer: Stop all payments to the suspicious party, gather evidence (messages, receipts), report to your bank, file a complaint on the national cybercrime portal/helpline, and inform the police. Acting quickly improves recovery chances.

english.question: Why do some loan apps ask for access to my contacts and photos?
english.answer: Legitimate lenders need only limited data for KYC and verification. Apps demanding access to contacts, gallery or messages are a red flag, as that data can be misused for harassment. Avoid such apps.

english.question: How do I build a strong loan profile over time?
english.answer: Pay every EMI and bill on time, keep credit utilisation low, maintain a long clean credit history, file ITRs regularly, and avoid frequent loan applications. Consistency builds a strong profile.

english.question: What is the smartest way to use a personal loan?
english.answer: Use it for genuine, planned needs (consolidating costlier debt, an emergency, an important expense) rather than discretionary spending, and borrow only what you can repay comfortably. This is general guidance, not financial advice.

english.question: Should I take a loan to invest in the stock market?
english.answer: Generally not advisable. Borrowing to invest adds risk - you owe fixed EMIs while returns are uncertain and can be negative. This is general information, not financial advice; consult a qualified advisor.

english.question: What is a CIBIL score (for salaried)?
english.answer: A CIBIL score is a 3-digit number between 300 and 900 that reflects your credit history and repayment behaviour. A higher score means better chances of loan approval. A score of 750 and above is considered very good. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: What is a CIBIL score (for self-employed)?
english.answer: A CIBIL score is a 3-digit number between 300 and 900 that reflects your credit history and repayment behaviour. A higher score means better chances of loan approval. A score of 750 and above is considered very good. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: What is a CIBIL score (for professionals)?
english.answer: A CIBIL score is a 3-digit number between 300 and 900 that reflects your credit history and repayment behaviour. A higher score means better chances of loan approval. A score of 750 and above is considered very good. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: What is the minimum CIBIL score required for a loan (for salaried)?
english.answer: Most banks prefer a score of 700-750 or above. NBFCs are often more flexible and may lend at 650-700, but usually at a higher interest rate. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: What is the minimum CIBIL score required for a loan (for self-employed)?
english.answer: Most banks prefer a score of 700-750 or above. NBFCs are often more flexible and may lend at 650-700, but usually at a higher interest rate. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: What is the minimum CIBIL score required for a loan (for professionals)?
english.answer: Most banks prefer a score of 700-750 or above. NBFCs are often more flexible and may lend at 650-700, but usually at a higher interest rate. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: How can I check my CIBIL score for free (for salaried)?
english.answer: You can get one free credit report a year from each bureau (CIBIL, Experian, Equifax, CRIF). Many banks and apps also show your score for free. Checking your own score does not lower it. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: How can I check my CIBIL score for free (for self-employed)?
english.answer: You can get one free credit report a year from each bureau (CIBIL, Experian, Equifax, CRIF). Many banks and apps also show your score for free. Checking your own score does not lower it. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: How can I check my CIBIL score for free (for professionals)?
english.answer: You can get one free credit report a year from each bureau (CIBIL, Experian, Equifax, CRIF). Many banks and apps also show your score for free. Checking your own score does not lower it. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: Does checking my score reduce it (for salaried)?
english.answer: No. When you check your own score it is a 'soft enquiry' and has no impact. Your score is only affected when a lender does a 'hard enquiry' on a loan application. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: Does checking my score reduce it (for self-employed)?
english.answer: No. When you check your own score it is a 'soft enquiry' and has no impact. Your score is only affected when a lender does a 'hard enquiry' on a loan application. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: Does checking my score reduce it (for professionals)?
english.answer: No. When you check your own score it is a 'soft enquiry' and has no impact. Your score is only affected when a lender does a 'hard enquiry' on a loan application. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: What should I do if my CIBIL score is low (for salaried)?
english.answer: Pay existing EMIs and credit card bills on time, keep credit utilisation below 30%, don't close old accounts, and avoid applying for many loans at once. Improvement usually takes 3-6 months. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: What should I do if my CIBIL score is low (for self-employed)?
english.answer: Pay existing EMIs and credit card bills on time, keep credit utilisation below 30%, don't close old accounts, and avoid applying for many loans at once. Improvement usually takes 3-6 months. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: What should I do if my CIBIL score is low (for professionals)?
english.answer: Pay existing EMIs and credit card bills on time, keep credit utilisation below 30%, don't close old accounts, and avoid applying for many loans at once. Improvement usually takes 3-6 months. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: Can I get a loan with no credit history (for salaried)?
english.answer: Yes, but it can be harder. New applicants can consider a secured loan, a co-applicant/guarantor, or a smaller loan amount. Timely repayment then helps build your credit history. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: Can I get a loan with no credit history (for self-employed)?
english.answer: Yes, but it can be harder. New applicants can consider a secured loan, a co-applicant/guarantor, or a smaller loan amount. Timely repayment then helps build your credit history. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: Can I get a loan with no credit history (for professionals)?
english.answer: Yes, but it can be harder. New applicants can consider a secured loan, a co-applicant/guarantor, or a smaller loan amount. Timely repayment then helps build your credit history. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: What is credit utilisation ratio (for salaried)?
english.answer: It shows how much of your credit card limit you are using. For example, spending 30,000 on a 1 lakh limit is 30% utilisation. Keeping it below 30% is good for your score. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: What is credit utilisation ratio (for self-employed)?
english.answer: It shows how much of your credit card limit you are using. For example, spending 30,000 on a 1 lakh limit is 30% utilisation. Keeping it below 30% is good for your score. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: What is credit utilisation ratio (for professionals)?
english.answer: It shows how much of your credit card limit you are using. For example, spending 30,000 on a 1 lakh limit is 30% utilisation. Keeping it below 30% is good for your score. For professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, etc.), your qualification and practice registration can unlock special schemes with better terms here.

english.question: Does a loan rejection affect my CIBIL score (for salaried)?
english.answer: A rejection itself does not directly lower your score, but each application triggers a 'hard enquiry' that can reduce it slightly. So avoid applying at many places at once. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: Does a loan rejection affect my CIBIL score (for self-employed)?
english.answer: A rejection itself does not directly lower your score, but each application triggers a 'hard enquiry' that can reduce it slightly. So avoid applying at many places at once. For self-employed borrowers, your ITRs (2-3 years), GST returns and business bank statements matter most here, since income is judged on business records.

english.question: What is the difference between settlement and full payment (for salaried)?
english.answer: Full payment means you repaid the entire loan. Settlement means the lender agreed to accept a lower amount - this shows as 'settled' in your report, which is a negative mark. A 'closed/paid' status is always better. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: How is loan eligibility decided (for salaried)?

For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: What is the age limit for a loan (for salaried)?

english.question: What is FOIR or the EMI-to-income ratio (for salaried)?
english.answer: FOIR is how much of your monthly income goes towards EMIs. Most lenders want total EMIs to stay within 40-50% of your income so that repayment stays safe. For salaried applicants, keep your salary slips and salary-credited bank statements ready, as steady monthly income makes this simpler.

english.question: What is the difference between a flat rate and a reducing rate (for salaried)?
